title: Price and Inflation Expectations Survey (PIES) - 2026 Q1 Report
summary: The Philadelphia Fed's Q1 2026 Price and Inflation Expectations Survey (PIES) shows Third District firms expect their own prices to rise 3.1% over the next four quarters, up from 2.6% in Q4 2025. U.S. inflation expectations held steady with a median of 3.0% and mean of 3.6%. Long-term median inflation expectations declined to 3.0% from 4.0%, while the mean rose to 7.4% from 6.0%, indicating disagreement among firms about future inflation trends.
key_points: Firms expect their own prices to rise 3.1% over the next four quarters (Q1 2026 to Q1 2027), up from 2.6% in Q4 2025
Expected U.S. inflation over next four quarters: median 3.0% (unchanged), mean 3.6% (unchanged)
Reported actual price increases over past year (Q1 2025 to Q1 2026): 2.8%, down from 3.0% last quarter
Long-term (10-year) median inflation expectation declined to 3.0% from 4.0%, while mean rose to 7.4% from 6.0%
Expected compensation costs per employee: 3.2%, little changed from 3.3% last quarter
Manufacturing firms reported 4.0% price growth (vs 2.9% nonmanufacturing) and expect 3.7% next quarter
why_it_matters: This survey provides forward-looking inflation expectations from actual businesses, offering real-economy input on pricing power and cost pressures. The divergence between median and mean long-term expectations suggests significant dispersion in firm-level inflation views, which could signal underlying uncertainty about the inflation trajectory.
macro_context: The survey reflects business sentiment from the Philadelphia Fed's Third District. Near-term own-price expectations rose while U.S. inflation expectations held steady, suggesting firms see localized pricing power without broader inflationary acceleration. The gap between median (3.0%) and mean (7.4%) long-term inflation expectations indicates significant disagreement among respondents.
notes: Source is a legitimate Federal Reserve survey article. Survey collected responses February 9-19, 2026. The long-term mean inflation expectation (7.4%) appears unusually high relative to median (3.0%), indicating outlier responses pulling the mean up. Confidence reduced from 7 to 5 due to regional (Third District) scope limiting broader applicability and inherent survey-based uncertainty.
